title: C++ Arrays
content:
### Arrays in C++

Arrays are fixed-size collections of elements of the same type stored in contiguous memory.

---

### Declaration and Initialization

```cpp
int numbers[5];                     // Uninitialized array of 5 ints
int scores[3] = {90, 85, 88};      // Initialized
int values[] = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};    // Size inferred from initializer
int zeros[10] = {0};               // First element 0, rest zero-initialized
```

---

### Accessing Elements

```cpp
int arr[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};

cout << arr[0];    // First element: 10
cout << arr[2];    // Third element: 30

arr[4] = 100;      // Modify last element
```

---

### Iterating Through Arrays

```cpp
int arr[] = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};

// Index-based
for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
  cout << arr[i] << " ";
}

// Range-based (C++11)
for (int x : arr) {
  cout << x << " ";
}
```

---

### Common Mistakes

| Mistake | Example | Problem |
|---------|---------|---------|
| Out-of-bounds | `arr[10]` when size is 5 | Undefined behavior |
| Array decay | Passing to function | Array becomes pointer |
| No bounds checking | No error on invalid index | Silent corruption |

---

> **Key Point:** Array indices start at 0 and go to size-1. C++ does not perform bounds checking - accessing out-of-bounds elements leads to undefined behavior.
